title: How to inform patients about side effects and risks | Managing Patient Expectations
filename: How to inform patients about side effects and risks ｜ Managing Patient Expectations.en.vtt
excerpt: So side effects from this is something I'm really worried about I think as a nurse we're just worried that we're going to hurt someone how do you warn someone about the risk of a vo the risk of swelling and bruising without scaring them up because I think whenever I say that to s…

segments.text: so side effects from this is something I'm really worried about I think as a nurse we're just worried that we're going to hurt someone how do you warn someone about the risk of a vo the risk of swelling and bruising without scaring them up because I think whenever I say that to someone I think how are they still trusting me to inject them when I've just told them these massive risks that could really impact their lives how do you say that without making someone Run for the door so um yeah there's definitely a skill in this and people go one of two ways in the beginning they either downplay it and don't give them enough information because they're worried which is very risky because if you do have a problem then the patient feels there's an injustice and that this is why you get sued is because the patient they will routinely come back and say I would never have had this treatment if I knew this could happen and of course that's not true because if you tell them they all have the procedure what they really mean is I didn't have the chance to avoid this and that feels like an injustice and that can be as simple as a bruise that's big there's quite a big bruise the trick is to is it's the story structure again you tell them the truth but not just the bad sides like it's not

just you might get a blocked artery which could blind you anaphylaxis and death yeah that's what I'm scared and that's what the other side of thing people do this on training courses I find it quite funny to be honest I'll say you guys practice consenting yeah and that's what we did on our foundation course I go yeah you can you might have an allergic reaction you might have asked your occlusion and there's a theoretical risk that you can have a stroke like wow yeah like they're terrified so it's they're leaving them having hanging them if you think of it from a story structure that's the maximum conflict of the moment which is now I've just told you all the bad things that can happen now you need to tell them the rest of the story which is what are the likelihood of those things and ideally like how can what can you compare that with so I know that your chance of getting in a car accident that causes a significant injury is about one in seventeen thousand so you could say you still drive every day and that is actually less likely to be injured in terms of blindness for sure than being a serious car accident but we all use so that helps them put it into some

perspective so you can help them get the scale right because this is another thing with the human brain without scale it sometimes just maximizes it and I think even injectors do this so they talk about for example non-surgical rhinoplasty as the riskiest place to inject so without a scale of exactly what that risk is if people just take it off the list as if it's an absolutely worth it yeah but actually the chances are still low and you can even lower it further by doing certain things that and that's the next thing I'd build into the consent process which is although there are these risks I'm doing a number of additional steps to try and reduce that risk and then it's really good now to actually teach them what you know about it so eyelid ptosis in the published data says one in a hundred chance of causing an eyelid to drop that's not the experience you can say that's not the experience of my trainer who has been who's taught me a specific technique to lower that risk and what I'll be doing as I'm injecting is putting some pressure on the orbit to stop Botox flowing in that direction I'll also be injecting at the right depths so I'll be injecting

um deeper in the middle but much more superficial as we get closer to the eyelid which means there are multiple layers of tissue that will defend that muscle from being affected by the Botox and using this technique I can tell you that my trainers had one injection in 10 years that caused a very slight eyelidosis that actually didn't need treatment so you can tell that kind of story with it so you're not just saying there's a risk of your eyelid dropping you're saying but the things I do what I'm doing and it's unlikely and then it's okay to at the end of that process you do the same with the best conclusion so the published data depending on which paper you read if ones I used to say one in fifteen thousand I found a paper that's pretty good there's one in six thousand you can then say one in six thousand mils equals the vascular collusion most of those are treated on the day and don't leave scarring but that's the thing we worry about that's why you need to understand it so you can come back and seek help but I will also check you before you leave so before you leave today I'll check value refill make sure that the blood flow is normal if I was to detect it to detect an abnormality I would dissolve it with this product that I have in my drawer I've got to hear I know how to use it yeah I had one day is you inject it you

massage The Vessel it can take um sometimes 45 minutes sometimes several hours depending on the type and the type of filler and where it is and I also have the backup of the people I've trained with who can advise over the phone yeah um and if I follow all that processes the chance of someone getting a scar who's diagnosed immediately is vastly lower than the ones who come back two days later those are the ones I worry about big bolus falsely reassured over the phone goes on for longer eventually they come back three days in you can't really help them that much so immediate diagnosis with a prepared clinician with a small vo it's actually pretty routine like you're not going to have that many problems doing that so you just have to inject in a way that causes if you're going to get a vo it's small if you're going to get one you diagnose it immediately and that you're always prepared for it and you're massively in a safer Camp so I basically teach them that and the more questions they ask you the better and in the beginning you'll be clunky because you haven't been asked all these questions but research it write a little mini paragraph in

response and eventually you become the person who brings certainty to the uncertainty so you do also bring the uncertainty because you say did you know you could go blind and cause a stroke well there are lots of ways I do things to prevent that so stroke for example you can explain that you need to put a huge volume in I don't think it's happened may have happened once with a with an aha but you typically are putting five mils of fat in on in a surgical procedure before what's called a filler causes a stroke so we're not using anywhere near that volume even if I was to directly inject the artery with the amount I'm going to put in it would never cause a stroke because it's not enough to get all the way down that vessel so you can tell them that stuff but I don't just rely on volume I also know about the depth I also get an aspirate before my treatment which will give me some percentage chance of detecting yeah so reduces the risk yeah so by the time they've heard you go through all the things that you do to make the risk safer compared with the person down the road who just waved the form in front of them read this yes yeah although it feels a bit scary because you're talking you're directly

talking about these things what they get is the opposite they're like this person actually knows what they're talking about it's actually a huge reason why people ended up having bigger procedures with me is I got so good at the The Confident delivery and breaking down of information but by the end of it they're like well if I'm gonna have it this is you know what you're doing you're keeping me safe and the patients who don't care about that are not a great patient base to build your practice if they only care about the price they're not interested in the technical details they just want to have it quickly done and they're actually not a great patient base and then all no disrespect to them we're all different but you want patients who value the safety and who value your time who value your expertise and we'll stick with you because of those things not someone who just wants it a little bit cheaper yeah well you're more than just a mill aren't you yeah [Music]
